schools.summary: The feast of trumpets and the holy seventh month — 'in the seventh month, on the first day... an holy convocation... a day of blowing the trumpets'; the month Tisri the most full of holy days of any in the year, the trumpets, day of atonement, and tabernacles all falling within it, having once been the first month before the Exodus made Abib the head, and so retaining a special sacredness (Gill, Henry); the new moon kept with an extraordinary blowing of trumpets, a memorial summoning the people to the solemn season opening; the chapter treating not the festivals themselves (given in Leviticus) but only the sacrifices by which their solemnization was graced (Calvin); the holy convocation calling the people to worship at the threshold of the holiest month.

themes: a day of blowing the trumpets
the richest month of holy days

schools.summary: The burnt offering of the feast of trumpets — 'a burnt offering for a sweet savour unto the LORD; one young bullock, one ram, and seven lambs of the first year without blemish' (the special burnt offering of the day, offered whole, with its accompanying meat offering of fine flour and oil, three tenth deals for the bullock, two for the ram, a tenth for each lamb, Keil and Delitzsch/Gill); 'without blemish' — every victim sound and unflawed, fit for God's altar; 'for a sweet savour' — the whole wholly consumed and ascending in flame, a fragrance well-pleasing to God, the trumpets summoning and the burnt offering consecrating the day; the typology — the unblemished victims pointing to Christ, who offered himself without spot to God (Heb 9:14).

themes: a burnt offering for a sweet savour
one bullock, one ram, seven lambs

schools.summary: The meat offering of the day — 'their meat offering shall be of flour mingled with oil, three tenth deals for the bullock, and two tenth deals for the ram' (and a tenth for each lamb), the meat offering measured to the victim, the largest portion for the bullock, less for the ram, least for each lamb, all of fine flour mingled with oil; the same quantity of flour and oil as in the meat offering at the new moons and the feast of unleavened bread, the rule of the accompanying offering holding here (Gill); the beast of the burnt offering never offered alone but with its meat offering, the fruits of the field joined to the firstlings, the whole produce of the land brought to God together; the fine flour and oil owning God as the giver of bread and the oil of gladness, pointing to Christ the bread of life anointed with the Spirit.

themes: their meat offering of flour and oil
three tenth deals for the bullock

schools.summary: The day's offerings added to the new-moon and daily sacrifices — 'beside the burnt offering of the month... and the daily burnt offering'; the special victims of the feast (a bullock, a ram, seven lambs) offered over and above two standing sacrifices — the burnt offering of the new moon, for this first day was also a new moon with its own offering (Num 28:11), and the continual daily burnt offering of morning and evening; 'the burnt offering of the month' meaning the offering of the new moon, belonging to every new moon (Ellicott, Wesley); the threefold worship of the day — the daily, the new-moon, and the feast offering, all on the one day, the trumpets-day falling on the new moon gathering the most abundant sacrifice; the extraordinary offerings not setting aside the ordinary but added to them, God's due rendered in full.

themes: beside the burnt offering of the month
offerings heaped on the standing sacrifice

schools.summary: The day of atonement — 'on the tenth day of this seventh month an holy convocation; and ye shall afflict your souls: ye shall not do any work' (the tenth of Tisri kept as a day of total rest and sacred assembly, like the weekly Sabbath); 'afflict your souls' meaning fasting, abstinence from all delightful things, and compunction of heart for sin and the judgments of God, the only day Israel was commanded to fast (Wesley); though the people never came before God without seeking pardon, on this day they in a special manner confessed their sins, the fast a token of guilt and humbling (Calvin); the fuller law given in Leviticus — the high priest entering the holiest with blood, the two goats (Lev 16, 23:26); the day between the trumpets that summoned to repentance and the tabernacles that crowned the year with joy; the typology — the clearest figure of Christ's expiation.

themes: the tenth day of the seventh month

schools.summary: The burnt offering of the atonement day — 'a burnt offering unto the LORD for a sweet savour; one young bullock, one ram, and seven lambs of the first year' (offered whole, with their meat offering of flour and oil, the same offering as on the first day of the month, Gill/Keil); this burnt offering over and above the great sin offering of the day — the bullock and two goats by which the high priest made atonement within the veil (Lev 16) — the festal burnt offering additional to that proper atonement service; Aben Ezra's opinion that the ram here was a different ram from that of the atonement ritual (Gill); even on the day of fasting and humbling, a burnt offering of entire consecration ascending, the affliction of the soul joined to the offering of the best; the typology — the unblemished victims pointing to Christ, the perfect offering through whom the atonement availed.

themes: a burnt offering for a sweet savour
one bullock, one ram, seven lambs

schools.summary: The meat offering of the atonement day — 'their meat offering shall be of flour mingled with oil, three tenth deals to a bullock, and two tenth deals to one ram' (with a tenth for each lamb), the meat offering of fine flour mingled with oil measured to each victim, the largest for the bullock, less for the ram, least for each lamb; the same quantity of flour and oil as on the first day of the month, the rule of the accompanying offering uniform, the grain offering always proportioned to the animal (Gill); the burnt offering never offered alone but with its meat offering, even on the day of fasting when the people ate nothing the offering of bread and oil brought to God; the fine flour and oil owning God as the giver of sustenance, pointing to Christ the bread of life; the fast a humbling before God, not a withholding from him.

themes: their meat offering of flour and oil
three tenth deals to a bullock
grain offering on the day of fasting

schools.summary: The sin offering of the day, besides the great atonement — 'one kid of the goats for a sin offering; beside the sin offering of atonement... and the continual burnt offering'; this kid a sin offering distinct from and additional to the goat by which the high priest made the special atonement of the day, the goat offered 'without' in the ordinary way, though of the same kind, a different victim from the goat whose blood was carried within the veil into the holiest (Lev 16:15, Gill); this added sin offering offered after the proper atonement service; the heaping of atonement upon atonement — on the very day of the great expiation yet another sin offering required, the day full of the confession of sin and the shedding of atoning blood; the continual burnt offering still beneath all; the more solemn the day, the more abundant the sin offering; the typology — every sin offering pointing to Christ, made sin for us (2 Cor 5:21).

themes: one kid of the goats for a sin offering
beside the sin offering of atonement

schools.summary: The feast of tabernacles, the joy after the fast — 'on the fifteenth day of the seventh month an holy convocation... ye shall keep a feast unto the LORD seven days'; soon after the day of atonement in which they afflicted their souls came the feast in which they were to rejoice, for those that sow in tears reap in joy (Ps 126:5, Henry); the chief of their festivals, dwelling in booths seven days, all the days offering sacrifices, the lesson that while we are in these earthly tabernacles we too must offer to God (Calvin, Wesley); a larger number of burnt offerings than any other feast — thirteen bullocks the first day, one fewer each day, with rams and lambs, seventy oxen in all over the seven days (Clarke, Ellicott); the dwelling in booths recalling the wilderness keeping, the ingathering crowning the year; the typology — God tabernacling with his people looking on to the Word made flesh who dwelt among us (John 1:14).

themes: the fifteenth day of the seventh month

schools.summary: The great burnt offering of the first day of tabernacles — 'thirteen young bullocks, two rams, and fourteen lambs of the first year; they shall be without blemish' (offered whole on the first day, with their meat and drink offerings, far more than the single bullock of the other feasts, the greatest of any feast, Gill/Keil); the descending number through the seven days — thirteen bullocks the first day, twelve the second, one fewer each day, down to seven on the last, seventy oxen in all over the week, the great and diminishing offering peculiar to this feast; 'without blemish' — every one of the many victims sound and unflawed; the joy of the ingathering and the wilderness remembrance overflowing in the most abundant sacrifice of the year; the typology — the unblemished victims in such number pointing to the fulness of the sacrifice of Christ, more than sufficient for all.

themes: the richest sacrifice of the year

schools.summary: The eighth-day solemn assembly — 'on the eighth day ye shall have a solemn assembly: ye shall do no servile work therein'; the day after the seven, not properly part of the feast of tabernacles (the booth-dwelling being for seven days only) but an appendage or closing festival joined to it (Gill); the word azereth, 'solemn assembly,' better 'closing feast day,' the same term used in Leviticus and Nehemiah (Lev 23:36, Neh 8:18, Ellicott); among the Jews esteemed the chief or high day of the whole feast, though fewer sacrifices offered, the people finishing the solemnity with a greater measure of joy and devotion (Clarke); 'no servile work' — like the first day, a holy day of rest and worship framing the feast at its end; the 'great day of the feast' on which our Lord cried 'If any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink' (John 7:37); the eighth day, beyond the seven, perhaps figuring the eternal rest.

themes: on the eighth day a solemn assembly
the chief day of the feast

schools.summary: The burnt offering of the eighth day — 'one bullock, one ram, seven lambs of the first year without blemish' (with their meat and drink offerings and a goat for a sin offering, the same offering as on the first day of the month, trumpets, and the tenth, atonement, Gill/Keil); the eighth day falling back from the lavish thirteen-to-seven bullocks of the feast week to a single bullock; through the seven days of tabernacles the bullocks descended from thirteen to seven, a great and diminishing offering, but on the eighth day, the closing assembly, only one bullock; though esteemed the chief day, its sacrifices the fewer, the greatness of the day lying not in the number of victims but in the joy and solemnity of the closing assembly; 'without blemish' — the victims, though few, sound and fit for God; the typology — the unblemished offering pointing to Christ, the one perfect sacrifice.

themes: one bullock, one ram, seven lambs

schools.summary: The appointed offerings distinguished from the voluntary — 'these things ye shall do unto the LORD in your set feasts, beside your vows, and your freewill offerings'; better 'these sacrifices shall ye offer at your set seasons' (Ellicott), 'set feasts' meaning rather the fixed and appointed times, since not all were feast days (the day of atonement was a fast), the verse gathering up all the stated offerings for the appointed seasons (Gill); 'beside your vows, and your freewill offerings' — these appointed, public, calendar sacrifices over and above whatever a man might offer privately in payment of a vow or of his own free devotion, the fixed worship not absorbing the voluntary but standing beside it; God appointing a settled round of public sacrifice binding on all, yet leaving room for the freewill gifts of love and the offerings of vows; the stated worship the duty of all, not crowding out the free offerings of a willing heart.

themes: these things ye shall do in your set feasts
beside your vows and freewill offerings

schools.summary: Moses' faithful declaring of the offerings — 'Moses told the children of Israel according to all that the LORD commanded Moses'; Moses repeating to the people all these laws concerning the sacrifices, with their additions and explanations, a faithful servant who delivered his Lord's whole message, holding nothing back (Gill); this verse forming the conclusion of the great list of public sacrifices set out in chs 28-29 — the daily, sabbath, new-moon, and all the yearly feasts, the whole calendar of Israel's stated worship gathered and sealed by Moses' declaration (Keil and Delitzsch); in the Hebrew Bible this verse beginning the thirtieth chapter (Ellicott); the faithfulness of the mediator — what God spoke to Moses, Moses spoke to Israel, exactly and fully; the people knowing the whole appointed order of their worship; all the offerings together rehearsing, in shadow, the one offering of Christ.

themes: Moses told the children of Israel
